name: Abnormal blistering of the skin
description: The presence of one or more bullae on the skin, defined as fluid-filled blisters more than 5 mm in diameter with thin walls.

name: Abnormal dermatoglyphics
description: An abnormality of dermatoglyphs (fingerprints), which are present on fingers, palms, toes, and soles.

name: Milia
description: Presence of multiple small cysts containing keratin (skin protein) and presenting as tiny pearly-white bumps just under the surface of the skin.

name: Thin skin
description: Reduction in thickness of the skin, generally associated with a loss of suppleness and elasticity of the skin.

name: Skin rash
description: A red eruption of the skin.

name: Thickened skin
description: Laminar thickening of skin.

name: Nevus flammeus
description: A congenital vascular malformation consisting of superficial and deep dilated capillaries in the skin which produce a reddish to purplish discolouration of the skin.

name: Telangiectasia of the skin
description: Presence of small, permanently dilated blood vessels near the surface of the skin, visible as small focal red lesions.

name: Cutis marmorata
description: A reticular discoloration of the skin with cyanotic (reddish-blue appearing) areas surrounding pale central areas due to dilation of capillary blood vessels and stagnation of blood within the vessels. Cutis marmorata, also called livedo reticularis, generally occurs on the legs, arms and trunk and is often more severe in cold weather.

name: Hypermelanotic macule
description: A hyperpigmented circumscribed area of change in normal skin color without elevation or depression of any size.

name: Subcutaneous nodule
description: Slightly elevated lesions on or in the skin with a diameter of over 5 mm.

name: Xanthomatosis
description: The presence of multiple xanthomas (xanthomata) in the skin. Xanthomas are yellowish, firm, lipid-laden nodules in the skin.

name: Subcutaneous lipoma
description: The presence of subcutaneous lipoma.

name: Aplasia cutis congenita over the scalp vertex
description: A developmental defect resulting in the congenital absence of skin on the scalp vertex, often just lateral to the midline.

name: Skin ulcer
description: A discontinuity of the skin exhibiting complete loss of the epidermis and often portions of the dermis and even subcutaneous fat.

name: Ectodermal dysplasia
description: Ectodermal dysplasia is a group of conditions in which there is abnormal development of the skin, hair, nails, teeth, or sweat glands.

name: Abnormality of the skin
description: An abnormality of the skin.

name: Premature skin wrinkling
description: The presence of an increased degree of wrinkling (irregular folds and indentations) of the skin as compared with age-related norms.

name: Palmoplantar hyperkeratosis
description: Hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the hand and the sole of the foot.

name: Reticular hyperpigmentation
description: Increased pigmentation of the skin with a netlike (reticular) pattern.

name: Macule
description: A flat, distinct, discolored area of skin less than 1 cm wide that does not involve any change in the thickness or texture of the skin.

name: Palmoplantar keratoderma
description: Abnormal thickening of the skin of the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet.

name: Papilloma
description: A tumor of the skin or mucous membrane with finger-like projections.

name: Papule
description: A circumscribed, solid elevation of skin with no visible fluid, varying in size from a pinhead to less than 10mm in diameter at the widest point.

name: Adenoma sebaceum
description: The presence of a sebaceous adenoma with origin in the sebum secreting cells of the skin.

name: Melanocytic nevus
description: A oval and round, colored (usually medium-to dark brown, reddish brown, or flesh colored) lesion. Typically, a melanocytic nevus is less than 6 mm in diameter, but may be much smaller or larger.

name: Mucosal telangiectasiae
description: Telangiectasia of the mucosa, the mucous membranes which are involved in absorption and secretion that line cavities that are exposed to the external environment and internal organs.

name: Multiple cafe-au-lait spots
description: The presence of six or more cafe-au-lait spots.

name: Acrokeratosis
description: Overgrowth of the stratum corneum characterized by flesh-coloured or slightly pigmented smooth or warty papules on the upper surface of hands and feet.

name: Skin tags
description: Cutaneous skin tags also known as acrochorda or fibroepithelial polyps are small benign tumours that may either form secondarily over time primarily in areas where the skin forms creases, such as the neck, armpit or groin or may also be present at birth, in which case they usually occur in the periauricular region.

name: Transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder
Aplasia/Hypoplasia of the middle phalanges of the hand
Abnormal form of the vertebral bodies
Abnormality of the cerebral white matter
Short middle phalanx of the 5th finger
Merkel cell skin cancer
description: A malignant cutaneous tumor of the elderly that is characterized by an aggressive course with regional nodal involvement, distant metastases and a high rate of recurrence. Most patients present with rapidly growing, painless, firm, non-tender, dome-shaped red, occasionally ulcerated skin nodules, which have a red or bluish color, measuring up to several centimeters, on predominantly sun-exposed areas of the body. The overlying skin is smooth and shiny, sometimes exhibiting ulcerative, acneiform or telangiectatic features.

name: Cutaneous photosensitivity
description: An increased sensitivity of the skin to light. Photosensitivity may result in a rash upon exposure to the sun (which is known as photodermatosis). Photosensitivity can be diagnosed by phototests in which light is shone on small areas of skin.

name: Erythematous macule
description: A macule (flat, distinct, discolored area of skin less than 1 cm wide that does not involve any change in the thickness or texture of the skin) with a red or reddish color often associated with inflammation or irritation.

name: Erythematous plaque
description: A plaque (a solid, raised, plateau-like (flat-topped) lesion greater than 1 cm in diameter) with a red or reddish color often associated with inflammation or irritation.

name: Regional abnormality of skin
description: An abnormality of the skin that is restricted to a particular body region.

name: Skin nodule
description: Morphologically similar to a papule, but greater than either 10mm in both width and depth, and most frequently centered in the dermis or subcutaneous fat.

name: Basal cell carcinoma
description: The presence of a basal cell carcinoma of the skin.

name: Squamous cell carcinoma of the skin
description: Squamous cell carcinoma of the skin is a malignant tumor of squamous epithelium.

name: Abnormal brain FDG positron emission tomography
Macular hypopigmented whorls, streaks, and patches
Congenital nonbullous ichthyosiform erythroderma
description: The term collodion baby applies to newborns who appear to have an extra layer of skin (known as a collodion membrane) that has a collodion-like quality. It is a descriptive term, not a specific diagnosis or disorder (as such, it is a syndrome). Affected babies are born in a collodion membrane, a shiny waxy outer layer to the skin. This is shed 10-14 days after birth, revealing the main symptom of the disease, extensive scaling of the skin caused by hyperkeratosis. With increasing age, the scaling tends to be concentrated around joints in areas such as the groin, the armpits, the inside of the elbow and the neck. The scales often tile the skin and may resemble fish scales.

name: Abnormality of blood and blood-forming tissues
Broad distal phalanges of all fingers
Malformation of the hepatic ductal plate
Single transverse palmar crease
description: The distal and proximal transverse palmar creases are merged into a single transverse palmar crease.

name: Redundant skin
description: Loose and sagging skin often associated with loss of skin elasticity.

name: Abnormality of the upper urinary tract
Poikiloderma
description: Poikiloderma refers to a patch of skin with (1) reticulated hypopigmentation and hyperpigmentation, (2) wrinkling secondary to epidermal atrophy, and (3) telangiectasias.

name: Neoplasm of the skin
description: A tumor (abnormal growth of tissue) of the skin.

name: Absent pigmentation of the ventral chest
description: Lack of skin pigmentation (coloring) of the anterior chest.

name: Bilateral single transverse palmar creases
description: The distal and proximal transverse palmar creases are merged into a single transverse palmar crease on both hands.

name: Erythema
description: Redness of the skin, caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin.

name: Pruritus
description: Pruritus is an itch or a sensation that makes a person want to scratch. This term refers to an abnormally increased disposition to experience pruritus.

name: Skin vesicle
description: A circumscribed, fluid-containing, epidermal elevation generally considered less than 10mm in diameter at the widest point.

name: Urticaria
description: Raised, well-circumscribed areas of erythema and edema involving the dermis and epidermis. Urticaria is intensely pruritic, and blanches completely with pressure.

name: Eczema
description: Eczema is a form of dermatitis. The term eczema is broadly applied to a range of persistent skin conditions and can be related to a number of underlying conditions. Manifestations of eczema can include dryness and recurring skin rashes with redness, skin edema, itching and dryness, crusting, flaking, blistering, cracking, oozing, or bleeding.

name: Lichenification
description: Thickening and hardening of the epidermis seen with exaggeration of normal skin lines.

name: Desquamation of skin soon after birth
Perioral eczema
description: A type of eczema that occurs in the lips and perioral area.

name: Recurrent skin infections
description: Infections of the skin that happen multiple times.

name: Seborrheic dermatitis
description: Seborrheic dermatitis is a form of eczema which is closely related to dandruff. It causes dry or greasy peeling of the scalp, eyebrows, and face, and sometimes trunk.

name: Acantholysis
description: The loss of intercellular connections, such as desmosomes, resulting in loss of cohesion between keratinocytes.

name: Hyperkeratosis
description: Hyperkeratosis is thickening of the outer layer of the skin, the stratum corneum, which is composed of large, polyhedral, plate-like envelopes filled with keratin which are the dead cells that have migrated up from the stratum granulosum.

name: Partial albinism
description: Absence of melanin pigment in various areas, which is found at birth and is permanent. The lesions are known as leucoderma and are often found on the face, trunk, or limbs.

name: Piebaldism
description: Piebaldism is characterized by stable and persistent, well-circumscribed depigmented patches present at birth affecting the skin of the face, trunk, and extremities in a symmetrical distribution.

name: Albinism
description: An abnormal reduction in the amount of pigmentation (reduced or absent) of skin, hair and eye (iris and retina).

name: Generalized abnormality of skin
description: An abnormality of the skin that is not localized to any one particular region.

name: Paronychia
description: The nail disease paronychia is an often-tender bacterial or fungal hand infection or foot infection where the nail and skin meet at the side or the base of a finger or toenail. The infection can start suddenly (acute paronychia) or gradually (chronic paronychia).

name: Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis
description: Recurrent or persistent superficial Candida infections of the skin, mucous membranes, and nails.

name: Dry skin
description: Skin characterized by the lack of natural or normal moisture.

name: Ichthyosis
description: An abnormality of the skin characterized the presence of excessive amounts of dry surface scales on the skin resulting from an abnormality of keratinization.

name: Diffuse telangiectasia
description: Telangiectases (small dilated blood vessels) with a diffuse localization.

name: Bruising susceptibility
description: An ecchymosis (bruise) refers to the skin discoloration caused by the escape of blood into the tissues from ruptured blood vessels. This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to bruising. The corresponding phenotypic abnormality is generally elicited on medical history as a report of frequent ecchymoses or bruising without adequate trauma.

name: Petechiae
description: Petechiae are pinpoint-sized reddish/purple spots, resembling a rash, that appear just under the skin or a mucous membrane when capillaries have ruptured and some superficial bleeding into the skin has happened. This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to developing petechiae.

name: Photoreceptor layer loss on macular OCT
Erythroderma
description: An inflammatory exfoliative dermatosis involving nearly all of the surface of the skin. Erythroderma develops suddenly. A patchy erythema may generalize and spread to affect most of the skin. Scaling may appear in 2-6 days and be accompanied by hot, red, dry skin, malaise, and fever.

name: Subungual hyperkeratosis
description: A thickening of the stratum corneum in the region beneath the nails.

name: Pustule
description: A small elevation of the skin containing cloudy or purulent material usually consisting of necrotic inflammatory cells.

name: Hypergranulosis
description: Hypergranulosis is an increased thickness of the stratum granulosum.

name: Keratosis pilaris
description: An anomaly of the hair follicles of the skin that typically presents as small, rough, brown folliculocentric papules distributed over characteristic areas of the skin, particularly the outer-upper arms and thighs.

name: Orthokeratosis
description: Formation of an anuclear keratin layer

name: Parakeratosis
description: Abnormal formation of the keratinocytes of the epidermis characterized by persistence of nuclei, incomplete formation of keratin, and moistness and swelling of the keratinocytes.

name: Dermal atrophy
description: Partial or complete wasting (atrophy) of the skin.

name: Increased bone density with cystic changes
Aplasia/Hypoplasia of the abdominal wall musculature
Hyperextensible skin
description: A condition in which the skin can be stretched beyond normal, and then returns to its initial position.

name: Atypical scarring of skin
description: Atypically scarred skin .

name: Prolonged neonatal jaundice
description: Neonatal jaundice refers to a yellowing of the skin and other tissues of a newborn infant as a result of increased concentrations of bilirubin in the blood. Neonatal jaundice affects over half of all newborns to some extent in the first week of life. Prolonged neonatal jaundice is said to be present if the jaundice persists for longer than 14 days in term infants and 21 days in preterm infants.

name: Spontaneous hematomas
description: Spontaneous development of hematomas (hematoma) or bruises without significant trauma.

name: Cutis laxa
description: Wrinkled, redundant, inelastic and sagging skin.

name: Hypopigmentation of the skin
description: A reduction of skin color related to a decrease in melanin production and deposition.

name: Skin plaque
description: A plaque is a solid, raised, plateau-like (flat-topped) lesion greater than 1 cm in diameter.

name: Verrucae
description: Warts, benign growths on the skin or mucous membranes that cause cosmetic problems as well as pain and discomfort. Warts most often occur on the hands, feet, and genital areas.

name: Squamous cell carcinoma
description: The presence of squamous cell carcinoma of the skin.

name: Freckling
description: The presence of an increased number of freckles, small circular spots on the skin that are darker than the surrounding skin because of deposits of melanin.

name: Palmoplantar hyperhidrosis
description: An abnormally increased perspiration on palms and soles.

name: Skin fissure
description: A clearly-defined and roughly linear cleavage in the skin that usually extends to the dermis.

name: Acne
description: A skin condition in which there is an increase in sebum secretion by the pilosebaceous apparatus associated with open comedones (blackheads), closed comedones (whiteheads), and pustular nodules (papules, pustules, and cysts).

name: Decreased level of D-mannose in urine
Erythema nodosum
description: An erythematous eruption commonly associated with drug reactions or infection and characterized by inflammatory nodules that are usually tender, multiple, and bilateral.

name: Pretibial blistering
description: A type of blistering that affects the skin of the tibial region.

name: Atrophic scars
description: Scars that form a depression compared to the level of the surrounding skin because of damage to the collagen, fat or other tissues below the skin.

name: Fragile skin
description: Skin that splits easily with minimal injury.

name: Palmoplantar blistering
description: A type of blistering that affects the skin of the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet.

name: Erythematous papule
description: A circumscribed, solid elevation of skin with no visible fluid that is reddish (erythematous) in color.

name: Hyperkeratotic papule
description: A circumscribed, solid elevation of skin with no visible fluid, varying in size from a pinhead to less than 10mm in diameter at the widest point that is composed of localized hyperkeratosis (the latter may be demonstrated histopathologically).

name: Oral mucosal blisters
description: Blisters arising in the mouth.

name: Skin erosion
description: A discontinuity of the skin exhibiting incomplete loss of the epidermis, a lesion that is moist, circumscribed, and usually depressed.

name: Conjunctival telangiectasia
description: The presence of small (ca. 0.5-1.0 mm) dilated blood vessels near the surface of the mucous membranes of the conjunctiva.

name: Pterygium
description: Pterygia are 'winglike' triangular membranes occurring in the neck, eyes, knees, elbows, ankles or digits.

name: Postnatal-onset ichthyosiform erythroderma
description: A type of ichthyosiform erythroderma with postnatal onset.

name: Premature graying of hair
description: Development of gray hair at a younger than normal age.

name: Cafe-au-lait spot
description: Cafe-au-lait spots are hyperpigmented lesions that can vary in color from light brown to dark brown with smooth borders and having a size of 1.5 cm or more in adults and 0.5 cm or more in children.

name: Decreased proportion of CD4-positive helper T cells
Epidermal nevus
description: Epidermal naevi are due to an overgrowth of the epidermis and may be present at birth (50%) or develop during childhood.

name: Epidermal acanthosis
description: Diffuse hypertrophy or thickening of the stratum spinosum of the epidermis (prickle cell layer of the skin).

name: Nevus
description: A nevus is a type of hamartoma that is a circumscribed stable malformation of the skin.

name: Axillary freckling
description: The presence in the axillary region (armpit) of an increased number of freckles, small circular spots on the skin that are darker than the surrounding skin because of deposits of melanin.

name: Plexiform neurofibroma
description: A neurofibroma in which Schwann cells proliferate inside the nerve sheath, producing an irregularly thickened, distorted, tortuous structure.

name: Inguinal freckling
description: The presence in the inguinal region (groin) of an increased number of freckles, small circular spots on the skin that are darker than the surrounding skin because of deposits of melanin.

name: Aplasia cutis congenita
description: A developmental defect resulting in the congenital absence of skin in multiple or solitary non-inflammatory, well-demarcated, oval or circular ulcers with a diameter of about 1 to 2 cm. Aplasia cutis congenita most commonly occurs on the scalp, but may present in the face, trunk, or limbs.

name: Congenital absence of skin of limbs
Erysipelas
description: Increased susceptibility to erysipelas, as manifested by a medical history of repeated episodes of erysipelas, which is a superficial infection of the skin, typically involving the lymphatic system.

name: Prominent superficial veins
description: A condition in which superficial veins (i.e., veins just under the skin) are more conspicuous or noticable than normal.

name: Congenital ichthyosiform erythroderma
description: An ichthyosiform abnormality of the skin with congenital onset.

name: Abnormality of prenatal development or birth
Defective DNA repair after ultraviolet radiation damage
Hypoplasia of mandible relative to maxilla
Partial agenesis of the corpus callosum
Hyperpigmentation of the skin
description: A darkening of the skin related to an increase in melanin production and deposition.

name: Follicular hyperkeratosis
description: A skin condition characterized by excessive development of keratin in hair follicles, resulting in rough, cone-shaped, elevated papules resulting from closure of hair follicles with a white plug of sebum.

name: Hypomelanotic macule
description: Hypomelanotic macules ("ash leaf spots") are white or lighter patches of skin that may appear anywhere on the body and are caused by a lack of melanin. White ash leaf-shaped macules are considered to be characteristic of tuberous sclerosis.

name: Abnormality of skin pigmentation
description: An abnormality of the pigmentation of the skin.

name: Progeroid facial appearance
description: A degree of wrinkling of the facial skin that is more than expected for the age of the individual, leading to a prematurely aged appearance.

name: Reduced subcutaneous adipose tissue
description: A reduced amount of fat tissue in the lowest layer of the integument. This feature can be appreciated by a reduced skinfold thickness.

name: Acanthosis nigricans
description: A dermatosis characterized by thickened, hyperpigmented plaques, typically on the intertriginous surfaces and neck.

name: Dermal translucency
description: An abnormally increased ability of the skin to permit light to pass through (translucency) such that subcutaneous structures such as veins display an increased degree of visibility.

name: Increased subcutaneous truncal adipose tissue
description: The presence of an abnormally increased amount of subcutaneous adipose tissue in the trunk of the body.

name: Congenital malformation of the left heart
Delayed closure of the anterior fontanelle
Periorbital hyperpigmentation
description: Increased pigmentation of the skin in the region surrounding the orbit of the eye.

name: Triangular shaped distal phalanx of the thumb
Retinal telangiectasia
description: Dilatation of small blood vessels of the retina.

name: Cyanosis
description: Bluish discoloration of the skin and mucosa due to poor circulation or inadequate oxygenation of arterial or capillary blood.

name: Fingerpad telangiectases
description: Telangiectasia (small dilated blood vessels) located in the fingerpads at the tips of the fingers.

name: Gastrointestinal telangiectasia
description: Telangiectasia affecting the gastrointestinal tract.

name: Lip telangiectasia
description: Telangiectasia (that is, the presence of small dilated superficial blood vessels) of the lips.

name: Nail bed telangiectasia
description: Telangiectases in the area of the nails.

name: Nasal mucosa telangiectasia
description: Telangiectasia of the nasal mucosa.

name: Palate telangiectasia
description: The presence of small (ca. 0.5-1.0 mm) dilated blood vessels near the surface of the mucous membranes of the palate.

name: Tongue telangiectasia
description: Telangiectasia (that is, the presence of small dilated superficial blood vessels) of the tongue.

name: Subcutaneous hemorrhage
description: This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to bruising (purpura, petechiae, or ecchymoses).

name: Telangiectasia
description: Telangiectasias refer to small dilated blood vessels located near the surface of the skin or mucous membranes, measuring between 0.5 and 1 millimeter in diameter. Telangiectasia are located especially on the tongue, lips, palate, fingers, face, conjunctiva, trunk, nail beds, and fingertips.

name: Scleroderma
description: A chronic autoimmune phenomenon characterized by fibrosis (or hardening) and vascular alterations of the skin.

name: Stiff skin
description: An induration (hardening) of the skin

name: Facial telangiectasia
description: Telangiectases (small dilated blood vessels) located near the surface of the skin of the face.

name: Psoriasiform dermatitis
description: A skin abnormality characterized by redness and irritation, with thick, red skin that displays flaky, silver-white patches (scales).

name: Left superior vena cava draining to coronary sinus
Abnormal proportion of CD8-positive T cells
Decreased proportion of CD4-positive T cells
Facial telangiectasia in butterfly midface distribution
description: Telangiectases (small dilated blood vessels) located near the surface of the skin in a butterfly midface distribution.

name: Cigarette-paper scars
description: Thin (atrophic) and wide scars.

name: Plantar hyperkeratosis
description: Hyperkeratosis affecting the sole of the foot.

name: Hyperkeratosis lenticularis perstans
description: Hyperkeratosis lenticularis perstans (HLP), also known as Flegel disease, is a keratinization abnormality characterized by small, asymptomatic erythematous papules that leave characteristic punctate bleeding when they become detached. The lesions generally occur symmetrically along the top of the foot and on the legs, appearing more rarely on the arms, forearms, palms, and soles, and even on the oral mucosa.

name: Osteolytic defects of the distal phalanges of the hand
Mottled pigmentation
description: Patchy and irregular skin pigmentation.

name: Anemic pallor
description: A type of pallor that is secondary to the presence of anemia.

name: Chromosomal breakage induced by crosslinking agents
Deficient excision of UV-induced pyrimidine dimers in DNA
Prolonged G2 phase of cell cycle
Scaling skin
description: Refers to the loss of the outer layer of the epidermis in large, scale-like flakes.

name: Skin dimple
description: Skin dimples are cutaneous indentations that are the result of tethering of the skin to underlying structures (bone) causing an indentation.

name: Chronic cutaneous wound
description: A cutaneous wound that has failed to proceed through the orderly and timely process to produce an atomic and functional integrity, or proceeded through the repair process without establishing a sustained anatomic and functional result.

name: Cutaneous melanoma
description: The presence of a melanoma of the skin.

name: Joint contractures involving the joints of the feet
Genital blistering
description: The presence of one or more bullae on the skin of the genital region, defined as fluid-filled blisters more than 5 mm in diameter with thin walls.

name: Atresia of the external auditory canal
Aplasia cutis congenita on trunk or limbs
description: A developmental defect resulting in the congenital absence of skin on the trunk or the limbs.

name: Aplasia cutis congenita over posterior parietal area
Acral blistering
description: Bullae (defined as fluid-filled blisters more than 5 mm in diameter with thin walls) of the skin with an acral distribution (affecting peripheral regions such as hands and feet)

name: Telangiectases in sun-exposed and nonexposed skin
Osteolytic defects of the phalanges of the hand
Recurrent bacterial skin infections
description: Increased susceptibility to bacterial infections of the skin, as manifested by recurrent episodes of infectious dermatitis.

name: Tapering pointed ends of distal finger phalanges
Cutaneous mastocytosis
description: Multifocal dense infiltrates of mast cells in cutaneous tissue.

name: Cone-shaped epiphyses of the phalanges of the hand
Avascular necrosis of the capital femoral epiphysis
Palmoplantar cutis gyrata
description: Cutis gyrata of palms and soles.

name: Palmoplantar cutis laxa
description: Loose, wrinkled skin of hands and feet.

name: Inflammatory abnormality of the skin
description: The presence of inflammation of the skin. That is, an abnormality of the skin resulting from the local accumulation of fluid, plasma proteins, and leukocytes.

name: Abnormal perifollicular morphology
description: Any structural anomaly in the areas surrounding the hair follicles.

name: Comedo
description: A clogged cutaneous sebaceous follicle, which is a cutaneous gland that secretes sebum (usually into a hair follicle).

name: Soft, doughy skin
description: A skin texture that is unusually soft (and may feel silky), and has a malleable consistency resembling that of dough.

name: Pallor
description: Abnormally pale skin.

name: Abnormality of bone marrow cell morphology
Abnormality of the periventricular white matter
Antecubital pterygium
description: Pterygium affecting the elbow. This is a cutaneous web that can lead to severe flexion contracture of the elbow joint. Antecubital pterygium can be unilateral, bilateral, symmetric, or asysmmetric.

name: Soft skin
description: Subjective impression of increased softness upon palpitation of the skin.

name: Striae distensae
description: Thinned, erythematous, depressed bands of atrophic skin. Initially, striae appear as flattened and thinned, pinkish linear regions of the skin. Striae tend to enlarge in length and become reddish or purplish. Later, striae tend to appear as white, depressed bands that are parallel to the lines of skin tension. Striae distensae occur most often in areas that have been subject to distension such as the lower back, buttocks, thighs, breast, abdomen, and shoulders.

name: Periarticular subcutaneous nodules
description: Subcutaneous nodules that are located in the vicinity of joints.

name: Cherry red spot of the macula
Foam cells in visceral organs and CNS
Diffuse reticular or finely nodular infiltrations
Chronic oral candidiasis
description: Chronic accumulation and overgrowth of the fungus Candida albicans on the mucous membranes of the mouth, generally manifested as associated with creamy white lesions on the tongue or inner cheeks, occasionally spreading to the gums, tonsils, palate or oropharynx.

name: Deep dermatophytosis
description: A type of invasive dermatophyte infection of the deep dermis characterized by extensive dermal infiltration by fungal elements.

name: Abnormality of pelvic girdle bone morphology
Palmar telangiectasia
description: The presence of telangiectases on the skin of palm of hand.

name: Plantar telangiectasia
description: Telangiectases (small dilated blood vessels) located on the skin of sole of foot.

name: Abnormal pigmentation of the oral mucosa
Multiple lentigines
description: Presence of an unusually high number of lentigines (singular: lentigo), which are flat, tan to brown oval spots.

name: Precocious puberty with Sertoli cell tumor
Loss of subcutaneous adipose tissue from upper limbs
Loss of truncal subcutaneous adipose tissue
Progressive loss of facial adipose tissue
Choroid plexus papilloma
description: Choroid plexus papilloma is a histologically benign neoplasm located in the ventricular system of the choroid plexus.

name: Discrete 2 to 5-mm hyper- and hypopigmented macules
Mottled pigmentation of the trunk and proximal extremities
Molluscoid pseudotumors
description: Bluish-grey, spongy nodules associated with scars over pressure points and easily traumatized areas like the elbows and knees.

name: Porokeratosis
description: A clonal disorder of keratinization with one or multiple atrophic patches surrounded by a clinically and histologically distinctive hyperkeratotic ridgelike border called the cornoid lamella.

name: Linear arrays of macular hyperkeratoses in flexural areas
Palmar hyperkeratosis
description: Hyperkeratosis affecting the palm of the hand.

name: Eruptive vellus hair cyst
description: A cutaneous cyst that is small (one or two millimeters in diameter) and painless, presenting as a follicular papule that usually is skin colored but may have a reddish or brownish tinge.

name: Epidermoid cyst
description: Nontender, round and firm, but slightly compressible, intradermal or subcutaneous cyst measuring 0.5-5 cm in diameter. Epidermal cysts are intradermal or subcutaneous tumors, grow slowly and occur on the face, neck, back and scrotum. They usually appear at or around puberty, and as a rule an affected individual has one solitary or a few cysts.

name: Folliculitis
description: Inflammatory cells within the wall and ostia of the hair follicle, creating a follicular-based pustule.

name: Steatocystoma multiplex
description: Multiple, localized or widespread, asymptomatic or inflammatory dermal cysts involving the pilosebaceous units. Lesions can appear anywhere on the body, but steatocystoma multiplex is more commonly involved with those areas of the skin with a high density of developed pilosebaceous units (e.g., the axilla, groin, neck, and proximal extremities).

name: Anterior synechiae of the anterior chamber
Abnormal size of the palpebral fissures
Abnormal isoelectric focusing of serum transferrin
Pilomatrixoma
description: Pilomatricoma is an asymptomatic slowly growing benign cutaneous tumor, differentiating towards the hair matrix of the hair follicle. It is covered by normal or hyperemic skin, and usually varies in size from 0.5 to 3 cm.

name: Patchy demyelination of subcortical white matter
Increased cellular sensitivity to UV light
Ivory epiphyses of the phalanges of the hand
Dense calcifications in the cerebellar dentate nucleus
Maculopapular exanthema
description: A skin rash that is characterized by diffuse cutaneous erythema with areas of skin elevation. It may evolve to vesicles or papules as part of a more severe clinical entity. Different degrees of angioedema with involvement of subcutaneous tissue may also appear.

name: Abnormality of epidermal morphology
description: An abnormality of the morphology of the epidermis.

name: Increased number of elastic fibers in the dermis
description: An elevated number of elastic fibers, that is of bundles of proteins and glycoproteins in the extracellular matrix in the reticular dermis. Elastic fibers can stretch and recoil back to their original length. This feature can be appreciated on histology with hematoxylin and eosin or other staining methods.

name: Perifolliculitis
description: Inflammation surrounding hair follicles.

name: Peripheral Schwannoma
description: The presence of a peripheral schwannoma.

name: Abnormal morphology of female internal genitalia
Thickening of the lateral border of the scapula
Disproportionate prominence of the femoral medial condyle
Sacral dimple
description: A cutaneous indentation resulting from tethering of the skin to underlying structures (bone) of the intergluteal cleft.

name: Plantar pits
description: The presence of multiple pits (small, pinpoint-large indentations on the surface of the skin) located on the skin of sole of foot.

name: Large clumps of pigment irregularly distributed along hair shaft
Purpura
description: Purpura (from Latin: purpura, meaning "purple") is the appearance of red or purple discolorations on the skin that do not blanch on applying pressure. They are caused by bleeding underneath the skin. This term refers to an abnormally increased susceptibility to developing purpura. Purpura are larger than petechiae.

name: Cutis marmorata telangiectatica congenita
description: A congenital vascular malformation that presents as localized or generalized erythematous-telangiectatic lesions with a reticular pattern; the lesions are almost always present at birth or develop in the first days of life. Cutis marmorata telangiectatica congenita (CMTC) appears as marble-like pattern (mottling) on the surface of the skin. In contrast to cutis marmorata, the marbling is more severe and always visible.

name: Telangiectases of the cheeks
description: Telangiectases (small dilated blood vessels) located near the surface of the skin of the cheeks.

name: Xanthelasma
description: The presence of xanthomata in the skin of the eyelid.

name: Poor wound healing
description: A reduced ability to heal cutaneous wounds.

name: Hair shafts flattened at irregular intervals and twisted through 180 degrees about their axes
Proximal muscle weakness in lower limbs
Proximal muscle weakness in upper limbs
Cutaneous leiomyoma
description: The presence of leiomyoma of the skin.

name: Cutaneous leiomyosarcoma
description: The presence of leiomyosarcoma of the skin.

name: Fibrofolliculoma
description: Fibrofolliculoma is a clinically asymptomatic, 2-4 mm, skin-colored, dome-shaped smooth papule. It usually arises in the form of multiple lesions in adults in different areas such as the scalp, forehead, face, and neck. According to histology, the lesion is a fibrotic hamartoma characterized by infundibular epithelial proliferation and perifollicular fibrous proliferation.

name: Trichodiscoma
description: A small benign fibrovascular tumor of the dermal part of the hair disk. Trichodiscoma is rather simple in appearance and consists of a dome-shaped fibrous tumor with a prominent vascular component that fills the papillary dermis under an atrophic epidermis. As in a normal hair disk, a hair follicle may be present at one edge of the papular lesion.

name: Capillary malformation
description: A capillary malformation is a flat, sharply defined vascular stain of the skin. It may cover a large surface area or it may be scattered and appear as little islands of color. In a capillary maformation, the predominant vessels are small, slow-flow vessels (i.e., arterioles and postcapillary venules).

name: Dermatographic urticaria
description: An exaggerated whealing tendency when the skin is stroked, that is, formation of red, itchy bumps and lines on the skin as a result of pressure on the skin (for instance, stroking the skin with a pen or tongue depressor).

name: Palmoplantar pustulosis
description: A chronic, relapsing, pustular eruption that is localized to the palms and soles.

name: Aplasia/Hypoplasia of the distal phalanges of the toes
Popliteal pterygium
description: A pterygium (or pterygia) occurring in the popliteal region (the back of the knee).

name: Axillary pterygium
description: Presence of a cutaneous membrane (flap) in the armpit.

name: Absent microvilli on the surface of peripheral blood lymphocytes
Reduced lymphocyte surface expression of CD43
Increased proportion of CD25+ mast cells
Jaundice
description: Yellow pigmentation of the skin due to bilirubin, which in turn is the result of increased bilirubin concentration in the bloodstream.

name: Giant melanosomes in melanocytes
description: The presence of large spherical melanosomes (1 to 6 micrometer in diameter) in the cytoplasm of melanocytes.

name: Facial erythema
description: Redness of the skin of the face, caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin.

name: Sclerodactyly
description: Localized thickening and tightness of the skin of the fingers or toes.

name: Civatte bodies
description: Eosinophilic hyaline ovoid bodies which are often found in the subepidermal papillary regions or sometimes in the epidermis. Civatte bodies (CBs) are seen as rounded, homogenous, eosinophilic masses on routine H and E staining lying in the deeper parts of epidermis/epithelium and more frequently in dermis/connective tissue. They are known as CBs (in epithelium/epidermis), colloid bodies, or hyaline bodies (in connective tissue). They are 10-25 micrometers in diameter and situated mostly within or above the inflammatory cell infiltrate. In lichen planus, the number of necrotic keratinocytes may be so large that they are seen lying in clusters in the uppermost dermis. These bodies show a positive periodic acid Schiff reaction and are diastase resistant

name: Yellow papule
description: A papule with yellow color.

name: Febrile seizure (within the age range of 3 months to 6 years)
Angiokeratoma
description: A vascular lesion defined histologically as one or more dilated blood vessels lying directly subepidermal and showing an epidermal proliferative reaction. Clinically, angiokeratoma presents as a small, raised, dark-red spot.

name: Hypoplastic dermoepidermal hemidesmosomes
description: Underdeveloped hemidesmosomes at the dermoepidermal junction. Hemidesmosomes are the specialized junctional complexes, that contribute to the attachment of epithelial cells to the underlying basement membrane in stratified and other complex epithelia, such as the skin.

name: Sebaceous gland carcinoma
description: A carcinoma that arises in a sebaseous gland (an exocrine gland of the skin that secretes sebum, a waxy substance)

name: Congenital giant melanocytic nevus
description: The giant congenital nevus is greater than 8 cm in size, pigmented and often hairy. A giant congenital nevus is smaller in infants and children, but it usually continues to grow with the child.

name: Nevus spilus
description: A tan, regularly bordered patch with darker macules within the lesion.

name: Lip pit
description: A depression located on a lip.

name: Low urinary cyclic AMP response to PTH administration
Broad distal phalanx of the thumb
Osteoma cutis
description: The term osteoma refers to the anomalous presence of ossification (bone formation) in the interior of the dermis or epidermis. The dermal or subcutaneous bone formation presents as stony hard nodules. The osteomata appear as irregular, hardened small nodules that are well circumscribed and generally of the same color as the skin.

name: Perifollicular hyperkeratosis
description: Increased amount of keratin (visible as white scales) surrounding hair follicles.

name: Deep palmar crease
description: Excessively deep creases of the palm.

name: Abnormality of the first metatarsal bone
Aplasia/Hypoplasia of the phalanges of the hallux
Linear nevus sebaceous
description: A type of nevus sebaceous with a linear form, raised borders and yellowish color.

name: Nevus sebaceous
description: A congenital, hairless plaque consisting of overgrown epidermis, sebaceous glands, hair follicles, apocrine glands and connective tissue. They are a variant of epidermal naevi. Sebaceous naevi most often appear on the scalp, but they may also arise on the face, neck or forehead. At birth, a sevaceous nevus typically appears as a solitary, smooth, yellow-orange hairless patch. Sebaceous naevi become more pronounced around adolescence, often appearing bumpy, warty or scaly.

name: Streaks of hyperkeratosis along each finger onto the palm
Abnormal distal phalanx morphology of finger
Multiple cutaneous leiomyomas
description: The presence of multiple leiomyomas of the skin.

name: Shagreen patch
description: A plaque representing a connective-tissue nevus. Connective tissue naevi are uncommon skin lesions that occur when the deeper layers of the skin do not develop correctly or the components of these layers occur in the wrong proportion. Shagreen patches are oval-shaped and nevoid, skin-colored or occasionally pigmented, smooth or crinkled, The word shagreen refers to a type of roughened untanned leather.

name: Hypoplasia of the abdominal wall musculature
Painful subcutaneous lipomas
description: The presence of multiple subcutaneous lipoma that cause pain.

name: Loss of subcutaneous adipose tissue in limbs
Abnormality of skeletal muscle fiber size
Increased adipose tissue around the neck
Melanin pigment aggregation in hair shafts
Congenital hypertrophy of retinal pigment epithelium
Decreased number of peripheral myelinated nerve fibers
Marked delay in eruption of permanent teeth
Benign neoplasm of the central nervous system
Molluscum contagiosum
description: Molluscum contagiosum is a cutaneous viral infection that is commonly observed in both healthy and immunocompromised children. The infection is caused by a member of the Poxviridae family, the molluscum contagiosum virus. Molluscum contagiosum presents as single or multiple small white or flesh-colored papules that typically have a central umbilication. The central umbilication may be difficult to observe in young children and, instead, may bear an appearance similar to an acneiform eruption. The lesions vary in size (from 1 mm to 1 cm in diameter) and are painless, although a subset of patients report pruritus in the area of infection. On average, 11-20 papules appear on the body during the course of infection and generally remains a self-limiting disease. However, in immunosuppressed patients, molluscum contagiosum can be a severe infection with hundreds of lesions developing on the body. Extensive eruption is indicative of an advanced immunodeficiency state.

name: Acne inversa
description: A chronic skin condition involving the inflammation of the apocrine sweat glands, forming pimple-like bumps known as abscesses.

name: Trichoepithelioma
description: A benign hair follicle tumor whose tumor cells form rudimentary hair follicles but not actual hair shafts. A trichoepithelioma is usually less than one centimeter, firm, round, and shihy with yellow, pink, brown, or bluish color. They may occur multiply, usually on the face, and may gradually increase in number with age.

name: Atopic dermatitis
description: Atopic dermatitis (AD) or atopic eczema is an itchy, inflammatory skin condition with a predilection for the skin flexures. It is characterized by poorly defined erythema with edema, vesicles, and weeping in the acute stage and skin thickening (lichenification) in the chronic stage.

name: Developmental stagnation at onset of seizures
Aplasia/Hypoplastia of the eccrine sweat glands
Cutis gyrata of scalp
description: The presence of convoluted folds and furrows formed from thickened skin of the scalp, resembling cerebriform pattern. The scalp has convoluted and elevated folds, 1 to 2 cm in thickness. The convolutions generally cannot be flattened by traction.

name: Flushing
description: Recurrent episodes of redness of the skin together with a sensation of warmth or burning of the affected areas of skin.

name: Periosteal thickening of long tubular bones
Ungual fibroma
description: Flesh-colored papule in or around the nail bed. Ungual fibromas may be periungual (arising under the proximal nail fold) or subungual (originating under the nail plate).

name: Neurofibromas
description: The presence of multiple cutaneous neurofibromas.

name: Abnormality of central somatosensory evoked potentials
Tendon xanthomatosis
description: The presence of xanthomas (intra-and extra-cellular accumulations of cholesterol) extensor tendons (typically over knuckles, Achilles tendon, knee, and elbows).

name: Tuberous xanthoma
description: A type of xanthoma characterized by a nodular form. Tuberous xanthomas are firm subcutaneous nodules,whereby the overlying skin can have red or red-yellow color changes.

name: Shortening of all phalanges of fingers
Cone-shaped epiphyses of the middle phalanges of the hand
Cone-shaped epiphyses of the proximal phalanges of the hand
Ivory epiphyses of the distal phalanges of the hand
Chronic furunculosis
description: A furuncle (boil) is a skin infection involving an entire hair follicle and nearby skin tissue. Chronic furunculosis refers to recurrent episodes of furuncles, often caused by recurrent staphylococcus infection.

name: Recurrent cutaneous abscess formation
description: An increased susceptibility to cutaneous abscess formation, as manifested by a medical history of recurrent cutaneous abscesses.

name: Decreased adipose tissue around neck
description: Reduced amount of adipose tissue in the region of the neck.

name: Metopic suture patent to nasal root
Connective tissue nevi
description: Connective tissue nevi are hamartomas in which one or several components of the dermis is altered.

name: Oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma
description: A squamous cell carcinoma that originates in the oropharnyx.

name: Aplasia cutis congenita of scalp
description: A developmental defect resulting in the congenital absence of skin on the scalp.

name: Atrophodermia vermiculata
description: Symmetrical vermiform facial atrophy that affects mainly the forehead, the chin, the ear lobes and helices. Atrophodermia vermiculata is characterized by erythema and follicular plugs on the cheeks, developing into painless reticular impressions.

name: Loss of gluteal subcutaneous adipose tissue
Abnormality of skin morphology
description: Any morphological abnormality of the skin.

name: Focal T2 hyperintense basal ganglia lesion
Spinal neurofibromas
description: Neurofibromas originating in the spine.

name: Subcutaneous neurofibromas
description: The presence of Neurofibromas in the subcutis.

name: Patchy palmoplantar keratoderma
description: A focal type of palmoplantar keratoderma in which only certain areas of the palms and soles are affected.

name: Deep plantar creases
description: The presence of unusually deep creases (ridges/wrinkles) on the skin of sole of foot.

name: Erlenmeyer flask deformity of the femurs
Midline skin dimples over anterior/posterior fontanelles
Aplasia/Hypoplasia of the phalanges of the 2nd toe
Macrodontia of permanent maxillary central incisor
Decreased number of large peripheral myelinated nerve fibers
Seborrheic keratosis
description: A raised growth on the skin of older individuals. The lesion usually is initially light tan and may darken to dark brown or nearly black. The consistent feature of seborrheic keratoses is their waxy, pasted-on or stuck-on look.

name: Pyoderma gangrenosum
description: A deep skin ulcer with a well defined border, which is usually violet or blue. The ulcer edge is often undermined (worn and damaged) and the surrounding skin is erythematous and indurated. The ulcer often starts as a small papule or collection of papules, which break down to form small ulcers with a so called cat's paw appearance. These coalesce and the central area then undergoes necrosis to form a single ulcer.

name: Abnormality of the fontanelles or cranial sutures
Decreased number of sternal ossification centers
Abnormality of the lower urinary tract
Edema of the dorsum of feet
Edema of the dorsum of hands
Neuronal loss in central nervous system
Functional abnormality of the gastrointestinal tract
Redundant neck skin
description: Excess skin around the neck, often lying in horizontal folds.

name: Decreased palmar creases
description: Poorly defined or shallow palmar creases.

name: Ecchymosis
description: A purpuric lesion that is larger than 1 cm in diameter.

name: Prominent fingertip pads
description: A soft tissue prominence of the ventral aspects of the fingertips. The term "persistent fetal fingertip pads" is often used as a synonym, but should better not be used because it implies knowledge of history of the patient which often does not exist.

name: Abnormal morphology of the choroidal vasculature
Actinic keratosis
description: A scaly, crusty lesion caused by damage from the ultraviolet radiation of the sun, with typical location on sun-exposed areas of the skin. Actinic keratosis lesions are often elevated, rough, and wartlike, and may be red, or occasionally tan, pink, or flesh-toned in color.

name: Absent skin pigmentation
description: Lack of skin pigmentation (coloring).

name: Decreased amplitude of sensory action potentials
Self-mutilation of tongue and lips due to involuntary movements
Abnormal palmar dermatoglyphics
description: An abnormality of the dermatoglyphs, i.e., an abnormality of the patterns of ridges of the skin of palm of hand.

name: Stenosis of the external auditory canal
Perifollicular fibrosis
description: Presence of excess fibrous connective tissue surrounding hair follicules.

name: Few cafe-au-lait spots
description: The presence of two to five cafe-au-lait macules.

name: Extension of hair growth on temples to lateral eyebrow
Long hairs growing from helix of pinna
Arteriovenous fistulas of celiac and mesenteric vessels
Premature delivery because of cervical insufficiency or membrane fragility
Abnormality of the middle ear ossicles
Venous varicosities of celiac and mesenteric vessels
Congenital bullous ichthyosiform erythroderma
description: An ichthyosiform abnormality of the skin that presents at birth or shortly thereafter with generalized erythema, blistering, erosions, and peeling. In the subsequent months, erythema and blistering improves but patients go on to develop hyperkeratotic scaling that is especially prominent along the joint flexures, neck, hands and feet.

name: Shortening of all distal phalanges of the fingers
Shortening of all distal phalanges of the toes
Bleeding with minor or no trauma
Fragmented elastic fibers in the dermis
description: Elastic fibers in the dermis exhibit an increased number of breaks associated with disorganization of the structure of the elastic fibers.

name: Unfavorable response of muscle weakness to acetylcholine esterase inhibitors
Curved linear dimple below the lower lip
Increased upper to lower segment ratio
Abnormality of the sense of smell
Short distal phalanx of the thumb
Prominent veins on trunk
description: Prominent thoracic and abdominal veins.

name: Contractures involving the joints of the feet
Angioedema
description: Rapid swelling (edema) of the dermis, subcutaneous tissue, mucosa and submucosal tissues of the skin of the face, normally around the mouth, and the mucosa of the mouth and/or throat, as well as the tongue during a period of minutes to several hours. The swelling can also occur elsewhere, typically in the hands. Angioedema is similar to urticaria, but the swelling is subcutaneous rather than on the epidermis.

name: Aplasia/Hypoplasia involving the central nervous system
Large cafe-au-lait macules with irregular margins
description: Large hypermelanotic macules with jagged borders.

name: Cutaneous myxoma
description: A myxoma originating in the skin.

name: Abnormal morphology of right ventricular trabeculae
Lichenoid skin lesion
description: Mutliple skin lesions resembling those characteristic of the disease lichen planus. These lesions are violaceous (reddish-purple), shiny, isolated, flat-topped papules and plaques.

name: Subcutaneous calcification
description: Deposition of calcium salts in subcutaneous tissue (i.e., the the lowermost layer of the integument).

name: Acral lentiginous melanoma
description: A type of cutaneous melanoma localized to the palm, sole, or beneath the nail (subungual melanoma). Acral lentiginous melanoma starts as a slowly-enlarging flat patch of discoloured skin and usually displays a size above 6 mm and often several centimetres or more in diameter upon diagnosis and variable pigmentation with a mixutre of colors including brown, and blue-grey, black and red. The surface of the lesion is initially smooth but later in the course may become thicker and irregular, and may ulcerate or bleed.

name: Gingival hyperkeratosis
description: Hyperkeratosis of the gingiva.

name: Morbilliform rash
description: An exanthema consisting of widespread pink-to-red macules (flat spots of 2-10 mm in diameter) or papules (red bumps) that blanch with pressure. The macules and papules may cluster and merge to form sheets over several days.

name: Tetralogy of Fallot with atrioventricular canal defect
Perioral hyperpigmentation
description: Increased pigmentation, either focal or generalized, of the skin surrounding the vermilion of the lips.

name: Absence of lymph node germinal center
Cobblestone-like hyperkeratosis
description: The presence of verrucous, cobblestone-like papules and nodules in a region of skin that is said to have an appearance like that of cobblestones.

name: Elevated circulating follicle stimulating hormone level
Excessive skin wrinkling on dorsum of hands and fingers
Multiple palmar creases
description: The presence of multiple creases on the palm of the hand (more than the normal three major creases (distal transverse crease, proximal transverse crease, and thenar crease).

name: Slender long bones with narrow diaphyses
Neonatal wrinkled skin of hands and feet

description: Depression located on the vermilion of the lower lip, usually paramedian.
Presence of telangiectases in the oral cavity.

Multiple verrucous lesions on the skin of the palm. These lesions are raised, have a thickened and rough surface, and may display prominent black dots (thrombosed capillaries). Palmar warts are caused by caused by human papillomavirus (HPV).

Multiple verrucous lesions on the skin of the sole of the foot. These lesions are raised, have a thickened and rough surface, and may display prominent black dots (thrombosed capillaries). Palmar warts are caused by caused by human papillomavirus (HPV).
A rudimentary tag of skin often containing ear tissue including a core of cartilage and located just anterior to the auricle (outer part of the ear).
A severe type of acne characterized by the formation of cysts enclosing a mixture of keratin and sebum.
A large nonpalpable spot of the skin over 1 cm in dimension with increased pigmentation and increased hair growth.
Erythema (Redness of the skin caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin) localized to the region surrounding the mouth.
Erythema (Redness of the skin caused by hyperemia of the capillaries in the lower layers of the skin) localized to the region surrounding the anus.
Urticaria caused by physical agents, such as heat, cold, light, friction.
Urticaria may be caused by cold temperatures.
Any manifestation of a skin disease associated with the production of pus.

Eruptive xanthomas are yellow-orange-to-red-brown papules that are often surrounded by an erythematous halo. They appear in crops on the buttocks, extensor surfaces of the extremities, and flexural creases. Acutely, variable amounts of pruritus and pain occur.
Jaundice that is sometimes present, sometimes not.
A large pigmented lesion measuring 5-15 mm in diameter with irregular, notched, and ill defined border and with color that may range from tan to dark brown to pink.
Depression located paramedially on the vermilion of a lip.
Depression located on the vermilion of the upper lip, usually paramedian.
Benign congenital lesions of the periauricular soft tissue consisting of a blind-ending narrow tube or pit.
A small, skin-lined tract that leads from the surface to deep within the tissues.

A systemic disease which can present with cutaneous lesions with or without internal involvement. Tumors are caused by Human herpesvirus 8 (HHV8), also known as Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV).
An abnormality of the region around the nails of the fingers or toes.
An abnormality of the plantar part of foot, that is of the soles of the feet.
A depression located at an oral commissure.
The formation of bullae (blisters) with cleavage in the lamina lucida layer of the skin.
A white or lighter patch of skin that may appear anywhere on the body and are caused by decreased skin pigmentation.
An infection of a hair follicle that extends subcutaneously, forming an abscess.
A type of epidermal nevus characterized by closely arranged, dilated follicular openings with keratinous plugs resembling classical comedones.
Benign congenital lesion of the postauricular soft tissue consisting of a blind-ending narrow tube or pit.
Small indentation anterior to the insertion of the ear.
A developmental defect resulting in the congenital absence of skin on the scalp in the parietal area.
Narrow, paramedian longitudinal depressions in the plantar skin of the forefoot.
A type of digital ulcer that manifests as an open sore on the surface of the skin at the tip of a finger or toe.
Multiple neurofibromas of the spinal nerve roots with a symmetric distribution.
Lack of skin pigmentation (coloring) of the chest.
Abnormal keratinization of the epidermal stratum coreum (horny layer) with increased keratin formation, preservation of the nuclei in the superficial cells, and absence of the stratum granulosum.
A type of acne in which open and closed comedones comprise the majority of the lesions, with substantially fewer papules and pustules.
Naevus flammeus localised in the skin of the forehead.

Cutaneous lesion that develops as a dry, scaly, red patch that evolves to an indurated and hyperpigmented plaque with adherent scale. Scarring may result in central white patches (loss of pigmentation) and skin atrophy.
Telangiectasia (that is, the presence of small dilated superficial blood vessels) of the oral mucosa.
An abnormality of the skin of the palm, that is, the skin of the front of the hand.
Loose and sagging skin of the fingers.
A severe degree of photosensitivity of the skin.
Presence of a cutaneous lipoma on the forehead.
Seborrheic dermatitis that is not localized to any one particular region.
The presence of unusually deep creases (ridges/wrinkles) on the skin of sole of foot located between the first and second toe.
Benign congenital lesion of the supraauricular soft tissue consisting of a blind-ending narrow tube or pit.
Small, hard cyst-like nodules, freely moveable in the subcutis over the bony prominences of the legs and arms, which have an outer calcified layer with a translucent core on x-ray.
A form of hyperkeratosis characterized by thickening of the cornified layer without retained nuclei.

Any structural anomaly of the elastic fibers of the skin. Elastic fibers are the essential extracellular matrix macromolecules comprising an elastin core surrounded by a mantle of fibrillin-rich microfibrils.

A congenital skin lesion characterized by irregular hypopigmented macules that coalesce to form plaques and occur particularly on the chest. It is generally present at birth or develops in the first days of life. It is more common in females. Diagnosis is confirmed by applying gentle friction to the lesion and the surrounding skin and checking that the erythema produced in the healthy skin does not appear in the hypopigmented lesion. This pale macule becomes more conspicuous when the lesion and its surroundings are rubbed. The margin of the naevus is ill-defined and consists of an archipelago of small anaemic spots.

A hollow mass located in the skin that is surrounded by an epithelium-lined wall and is well demarcated from the adjacent tissue. Cysts are often said to be sac-like and may contain serous liquid or semisolid material.

A congenital subcutaneous cyst that arises from entrapment of skin along the lines of embryonic fusion. In contrast to epidermal cysts, dermoid cysts tend to contain various adnexal structures such as hair, sebaceous, eccrine or apocrine glands. Dermoid cysts are present at birth, and are indolent, firm, deep, subcutaneous nodules. They are often located on the head and neck, and rarely in the anogenital area. Dermoid cysts arenslowly progressive and can grow to a size of 1 to 4 cm.
An erythematous (red), flat facial rash that affects the skin in the malar area (over the cheekbones) and extends over the bridge of the nose.
An area of fat accumulation at the back of the neck in the form of a hump.

Flesh-colored or yellowish papules, 2 mm or larger, that are responses to internal mechanical pressure and weakness in the connective tissue in the dermis, appear commonly over the medial aspect of the heel, but in some cases on the wrists. They are thought to represent herniations of adipose tissue through the plantar fascia retinaculum.
Loss of the outer layer of the epidermis in large, scale-like flakes localized to the palm of the hand and the sole of the foot.

A skin lesions that resembles the lesions observed in psoriasis, viz., an erythematous plaque covered by fine silvery scales. Psoriasiform lesions can be observed in psoriasis as well as in other conditions including allergic contact dermatitis, seborrhoeic dermatitis, Atopic dermatitis, pityriasis rubra, and lichen simplex chronicus.
Slightly raised wart 2-5 mm in diameter often associated with viral infections, commonly persistent in immunodeficient individuals.
Neurofibromas present in a smaller number than usually seen in neurofibromatosis type 1.

Chilblains, also called perniosis, are an inflammatory skin condition related to an abnormal vascular response to the cold. We are unaware of a reliable estimate of incidence. It typically presents as tender, pruritic red or bluish lesions located symmetrically on the dorsal aspect of the fingers, toes, ears and nose. Less commonly, reports describe involvement of the thighs and buttocks. The lesions present hours after exposure to cold and usually resolve spontaneously in one to three weeks.
A pterygium (or pterygia) in the intercrural (groin) region.
Pterygia affecting the neck.
Excessive wrinkling of the skin of the face.
The presence of an abscess located around the anus.
Absence or underdevelopment of the palmar creases.
The absence of the major creases of the palm (distal transverse crease, proximal transverse crease, or thenar crease).
An abnormality of the creases of the skin of palm of hand.
Any abnormal increase or reduction in skin elasticity.

Nodular changes affecting the eyelids may have many different causes such as cystic lesions (chalaziae, hordeolae), lipogranulomas, melanomas, infectious diseases (Molluscum contagiosum) and many more.
Naevus flammeus localised in the skin of the eyelid.
Any abnormality of the physiological function of the skin.
An abnormality of the melanosomes, i.e., of the cellular organelles in which melanin pigments are synthesized and stored within melanocytes (the cells that produce pigment in the dermis).
A soft tissue prominence of the ventral aspects of the fingertips or toe tips.
A crease that connects the proximal and distal transverse palmar creases.
Extension of the proximal transverse crease (five finger crease) to the ulnar edge of the palm.
A lesion of the skin that is located in a specific region rather than being generalized.
Thickening of the epidermal layer of the skin.
Congenital deep dermal melanosis in the sacral area.
Increased susceptibility to cutaneous fungal infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of cutaneous fungal infections.

A subtype of melanoma in situ that typically develops on sun-damaged skin. The lesion is typically a large, irregularly pigmented macule that has developed from an ordinary lentigo (a small pigmented spot on the skin with a clearly-defined edge). Change to a malignant lentigo typically takes place over 20 years or more, and many patients accept the change as a consequence of aging.
Recurrent infection involving the vulva, vagina, and adjacent crural areas, whereby the causative agent belongs to the genus Candida.

A form of telangiectasis characterized by a central elevated red dot the size of a pinhead, representing an arteriole, with numerous small blood vessels that radiate out thereby resembling the legs of a spider. Characteristically, compression of the central arteriole causes the entire lesion to blanch, and the lesion quickly refills once the compression is released.
A benign or malignant neoplasm that arises from the hair follicles, sebaceous glands, or sweat glands.
An uncontrolled autonomous cell-proliferation originating in a hair follicle, which is an epidermal adnexal structures responsible for hair growth.
A benign tumour originating from the outer root sheath of the hair follicle.
Increase in diameter of the veins located underneath the skin of the abdomen.
A well-circumscribed, intensely pruritic, raised wheal (edema of the superficial skin) typically 1 to 2 cm in diameter.
An atrophic scar (fibrous connective tissue resulting from incomplete healing of a wound) that has stretched (gotten wider), a manifestation of tissue fragility.
Numerous lymphocytes surrounding blood vessels in the superfical part of the dermis.

Ichthyosis follicularis is characterized by widespread non inflammatory thorn-like follicular projections. Dyskeratotic papules are most pronounced over the extensor extremities and scalp and are symmetrically distributed.
A circumscribed area of pus or necrotic debris in the skin.
Purpura that is flat (non-palpable, not raised).

A benign, well circumscribed sweat gland neoplasm with eccrine or apocrine differentiation. It usually presents as a solitary, dome-shaped papule, nodule, or plaque on acral sites. It is characterized by a proliferation of uniform basaloid cells in the dermis and it is associated with the presence of focal ductal and cystic structures [NCIT:C27273].
A cystic lesions that forms a benign tumor of an apocrine sweat gland.

Any anomaly of the structure of the acellular zone that is between the dermis and the epidermis and which functions to bind the epidermis to the dermis and to serve as a selective barrier allowing the control of molecular and cellular exchanges between the two compartments.
Presence of IgG antibodies in the dermoepidermal junction that are distributed in a linear pattern. This feature can be appreciated by immunofluorescence microscopy.
An open sore on the surface of the skin of a finger or toe.
A softening and breaking down of skin resulting from prolonged exposure to moisture. Macerated skin becomes soft and wrinkly and takes on a whitish hue.
Loss of sections of skin either spontaneously or after gentle handling.
A type of infection of the skin that can be regarded as a sign of a pathological susceptibility to infection.
An abnormal structure or appearance of hemidesmosomes, multiprotein complexes that facilitate the stable adhesion of basal epithelial cells to the underlying basement membrane.
Infection that extends deeply into the dermins by dermatophytes, fungi that typically cause different types of superficial infection (tinea) or skin, hair, or nails.
Persistent blue color of the skin that surrounds the mouth.

A cutaneous wound is a defined as a disruption of normal anatomic structure and function of the skin that occured owing to an injury of the skin. Wound healing is a dynamic, interactive processinvolving soluble mediators, blood cells, extracellularmatrix, and parenchymal cells. Wound healing has three phases: inflammation, tissue formation, and tissue remodeling, that overlap in time.

With the pathergy test, a small, sterile needle is inserted into the skin of the forearm. The site of injectionis circuled and observed after one and two days. If a small red bump or pustule at the site of needle insertion occurs, the pathergy test is considered to have a positive (abnormal) result.
Pruritus is an itch or a sensation that makes a person want to scratch. This term refers to an abnormally increased sensation of itching over the skin of the foot.
Pruritus is an itch or a sensation that makes a person want to scratch. This term refers to an abnormally increased sensation of itching over the skin of the abdomen.
A sensation of itching in the vulvar region.
Pruritus is an itch or a sensation that makes a person want to scratch. This term refers to an abnormally increased sensation of itching over the skin of the hand.
Pruritus is an itch or a sensation that makes a person want to scratch. This term refers to an abnormally increased sensation of itching over the palm(s) of the hand.
Pruritus is an itch or a sensation that makes a person want to scratch. This term refers to an abnormally increased sensation of itching over the skin of the breast.
A sensation of itching in the vagina.

Majocchi's granuloma (MG) is an inflammatory and granulomatous, dermatophytic infection characterized by a granulomatous inflammation around the hair follicle. Histopathologically, MG demonstrates a nodular perifollicular granulomatous infiltrate of lymphoid cells, macrophages, epithelioid cells, multinucleated giant cells, and neutrophils. Unlike superficial dermatophytoses, fungal hyphae and spores can be detected not only on the surface of the epidermis but also within or around the hair follicles.
A type of invasive dermatophyte infection characterized by vascular involvement and dissemination to other organs.
The presense of molluscum contagiosum lesions across multiple areas of the body.
The Harlequin phenomenon consists of a sudden change in skin colour, resulting in two different body colours, one on each half of the body.
Presence of abnormally increased amounts of intraepidermal inflammatory cells with a predominance of eosinophils.
In a heliotrope rash, the color of the skin turns to violet, which is the color of the heliotrope flower.
Heliotrope rash is a violaceous discoloration of the eyelids associated with periorbital edema.
A cutaneous eruption that consists of multiple (at least two) concentric erythematous rings.
A hair-containing cyst or sinus usually in the coccygeal region.

An inflammatory process in skin caused by an exogenous agent that directly or indirectly injure the skin. If the offending agent is identified and removed, the eruption will resolve. An unusual or patterned eruption may be a clue to the presence of a contact dermatitis. Patch testing may be helpful in the differential diagnosis.
A form of atopic dermatitis with onset in adulthood characterized by atopic red face, chronic lichenified eczema on the trunk, subacute or psoriasiform dermatitis.

A type of eczema characterized by pruritic coin-shaped patches on the skin. Nummular eczema most commonly occurs on the extremities, particularly the legs, but may occur anywhere on the trunk, hands, or feet
Persistent overgrowth of Candida albicans in the gastrointestinal tract.
Increased susceptibility to viral skin infections, as manifested by recurrent episodes of viral skin infections.
A predominantly neutrophilic infiltrate of the dermis and or epidermis (i.e., a large number of neutrophils inferred to have migrated into the skin).
Collection of neutrophils in the epidermis.
Collection of neutrophils in the dermis.
Numerous lymphocytes surrounding blood vessels in the deep part of the dermis.
A group of infected hair follicles, larger and deeper than a furuncle.
A reduced ability of the skin of the fingertips to wrinkle when exposed to stimuli such as soaking in water or application of EMLA cream (the fingertip remains smooth).

Keratoacanthoma (KA) is a common benign epithelial tumour that originates from the pilosebaceous glands. In most cases, it is characterized by rapid evolution, followed by spontaneous resolution over 4 to 6 months. KA usually presents as a solitary flesh-coloured nodule with a central keratin plug on the sun-exposed skin of elderly individuals.

Lymphocytoma cutis, or Spiegler-Fendt sarcoid, is classed as one of the pseudolymphomas, referring to inflammatory disorders in which the accumulation of lymphocytes on the skin resemble, clinically and histopathologically, cutaneous lymphomas. Careful clinical evaluation, histopathological and immunohistochemical exams may be needed to make the correct diagnosis.

Eccrine syringofibroadenoma (ESFA) is a benign adnexal tumor arising most often on the extremities of elderly individuals characterized by anastomosing cords of cuboidal epithelial cells surrounded by a fibrovascular stroma containing plasma cells and ductal structures. ESFA stains positively with epithelial membrane antigen (EMA) and carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA).
Multiple skin warts located in multiple parts of the body, e.g., neck, trunks, and extremities.

Warts affecting the skin in the genital area (peniile shaft, scrotum, vagina, or labia majora). Warts can be small, beginning as a pinhead-size swelling that may become larger and take on a pdenuculated appearance. Warts can spread and coalesce into large masses in the genital or anal area. Their color is variable but tends to be skin colored or darker, and they may occasionally bleed. Warts may cause itching, redness, or discomfort. An outbreak of genital warts may also cause psychological distress.
A benign epithelial neoplasm characterized by a papillary growth pattern and a proliferation of neoplastic squamous cells without morphologic evidence of malignancy [NCI thesaurus].
A benign exophytic neoplasm that arises from the oropharynx. It is characterized by the presence of a connective tissue core covered by stratified squamous epithelium [NCI thesaurus].
A benign skin adnexal tumor of eccrine differentiation.
Presence of multiple trichilemmomata, a benign tumour originating from the outer root sheath of the hair follicle.
Presence of a unitary trichilemmoma, a benign tumour originating from the outer root sheath of the hair follicle.

A cutaneous adnexal neoplasm with variable clinical presentation. It tends to be located in the head and neck and the presentation is papulonodular, scaly, asymptomatic, measuring up to 1-2cm, simulating a basal cell carcinoma.
A benign epithelial skin tumor manifesting as a slightly elevated circular plaque or nodule with a red, pink or brown color and a diameter up to 22 mm.

Perifollicular fibroma is a rare cutaneous hamartoma that shows differentiation in the connective tissue sheath of hair follicles. It can occur as a solitary papule or as multiple lesions. Histologically, the lesion consists of a concentric arrangement of cellular fibrous tissue around a normal hair follicle.
A type of melanoma that is flat and irregular in shape and color, with different shades of black and brown.
A type of melanoma that starts as a raised area that is usually dark blackish-blue or bluish-red but may not have any color.
Redness surrounding the hair follicles.
Loss of the outer layer of the epidermis in large, scale-like flakes localized to one or more fingertips.
A type of epidermal acanthosis characterized by a jagged (sawtooth) appearance of the rete ridges of the epidermis.

Rete pegs (or ridges) are the epithelial extensions that project into the underlying connective tissue in both skin and mucous membranes. Rete ridge flattening refers to the loss of these projections so that the skin epithelium acquires a relatively flat appearance.
Formation of clumps or aggregates that make up small protuberances from elastic fibers within the dermis (especially the reticular dermis).
An increase of the diameter of elastic fibers in the dermis.
An increased amount of desmosine measure in the skin. Desmosine is a cross-linking amino acid formed from lysyl residues in elastin.

Brown or blue-gray discoloration of the skin that can present on the axillary and inguinal areas, face, palms or soles. In addition, blue-black discoloration can be apparent on skin overlying cartilage in which the pigment is deposited, such as the ears. This is a characteristic manifestation of alkaptonuria, which is an autosomal recessively inherited deficiency of homogentisic acid oxidase that results in accumulation of homogentisic acid in collagenous structures. The sclerae are also typically involved.

A dermatosis characterized by generalized shiny, enamel-like, hyperpigmented scales in an irregular pattern. The scales may peel or desquamate, rather like old, sun-baked blistered paint, often with areas of underlying hypopigmentation. This has led to the terms peeling paint or flaky paint dermatosis (See the Figure in PMID:24285001).
A skin lesion with a snake- or serpent-like distribution.
A solid, raised, plateau-like (flat-topped) lesion greater than 1 cm in diameter and that has a yellow color.
A solid, raised, plateau-like (flat-topped) lesion greater than 1 cm in diameter that is characterized by hardening (sclerosis) of the affected skin area (related to collagen thickening).
